takeaway: Learn ==10 words== and every AI headline starts making sense.

terms.definition: Software that can do things we usually think of as needing human intelligence — understanding language, recognizing images, making decisions. It's not a robot brain; it's very advanced pattern-matching.
Building software by showing it lots of examples instead of writing exact rules. Like teaching a child what a cat is by showing pictures, not by describing whiskers and ears.
AI that creates new things — text, images, music, video — rather than just sorting or labeling existing ones. ChatGPT (text) and Midjourney (images) are generative AI.

The technology behind chatbots like ChatGPT and Claude. It has 'read' an enormous amount of text and learned to predict which words come next — which turns out to be enough to write, summarize, translate, and answer questions.
The 'brain' itself — the program that comes out of training. Companies release models with names and versions (GPT-5, Claude, Gemini) the way carmakers release car models.
The examples an AI learned from — for chatbots, huge amounts of text from books, websites, and articles. An AI can only be as good (or as biased) as what it learned from.
The small chunks of text an AI reads and writes — usually a word or piece of a word ('strawberry' might be two or three tokens). AI limits and prices are usually counted in tokens.
An app that lets you talk with an AI in a back-and-forth conversation, like texting. ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini are chatbots built on top of large language models.
Whatever you type to an AI — your question or instruction. Specific prompts with context get better answers, just like requests to a helpful colleague.
When an AI confidently states something false — a made-up fact, quote, or source. It isn't lying on purpose; it's producing plausible-sounding words. Always double-check facts that matter.
An AI that handles more than text — it can also look at images, listen to audio, or watch video. Most major chatbots are now multimodal.
Models that 'think out loud' step by step before answering, spending extra time on hard problems. Usually better at math, logic, and planning — but slower.
An AI that doesn't just answer questions — it takes actions to finish a task: browsing the web, filling in forms, writing and running code, often across many steps with little supervision.
How much a chatbot can 'keep in mind' at once — your conversation plus any documents you've shared. When a chat gets very long, the oldest parts can fall out of its working memory.

learnMore.label: IBM: What is a context window?

terms.definition: Hidden instructions an app gives the AI before you start typing — its job description: 'You are a helpful assistant. Be concise. Don't give medical diagnoses.'
A setting that controls how predictable or adventurous an AI's answers are. Low temperature = consistent and safe; high temperature = varied and creative. (Try the dial below.)
The moment an AI is actually running to produce your answer — as opposed to training, when it was learning. Every reply you get is 'inference.'
The internal dials — millions to trillions of them — that get tuned during training and store what a model has learned. Roughly: more parameters, bigger model.
The craft of phrasing requests so the AI gives its best results — being specific, giving examples, and providing background. Less magic than the name suggests: mostly clear communication.
A technique where the AI looks things up in a chosen set of documents before answering — an open-book exam instead of answering from memory. It makes answers more accurate and easier to verify.
Giving an already-trained model extra training on specific examples so it gets better at one particular job — like a general doctor going on to specialize.
When an AI's answers unfairly favor or disfavor certain groups or viewpoints. It happens because the AI learned from human-written text that carried those same biases.
The rules and filters built around an AI to prevent harmful output — the reason a chatbot sometimes says 'I can't help with that.'
Open(-weight) models, like Meta's Llama, can be downloaded and run by anyone. Closed models, like GPT-5 or Claude, can only be used through the company's own apps and services.
A password that lets a program (rather than a person) use an AI service. It's how developers plug AI into their own apps — and how the usage gets billed.
Letting an AI use tools mid-answer: it decides 'I should search the web' or 'I should run this calculation,' does it, and works the result into its reply.
An open standard, started by Anthropic, that lets AI assistants connect to outside tools and data — files, email, calendars — through one common plug. Think USB-C, but for AI.
Caps on how often you or your app can call an AI service in a given time window — the reason heavy use sometimes hits a 'please try again later.'
How long you wait for the AI's answer. Bigger and 'reasoning' models are usually smarter but slower — a real trade-off when building apps.
AI services charge per token, usually with different rates for what you send in and what the model writes back — like a taxi meter for text.
A way of turning text into lists of numbers that capture meaning, so a computer can tell that 'dog' and 'puppy' are related. The foundation of AI-powered search.
A database built to store and search embeddings — so you can ask 'find documents that mean something similar to this' instead of matching exact words.
Two ways to get better answers. Prompt engineering changes what you ask — cheap and instant. Fine-tuning changes the model itself — expensive, for repeated specialized tasks. Try prompting first.
Designing everything the model sees — instructions, documents, tool results, conversation history — not just the question. The successor to prompt engineering for people building AI apps.
The 'conductor' code around an AI app: sending prompts, calling tools, chaining several AI steps together, and handling errors along the way.
The actual numbers inside a trained model — the values of all its parameters. 'Releasing the weights' means letting anyone download and run the model themselves.
Cloud models run on the company's servers, so your data travels to them. Local models run on your own computer — private and offline-capable, but usually less powerful.
